skill.name: lark-shared
skill.description: 飞书/Lark CLI shared foundation for app configuration initialization, authentication login, identity switching, permission and scope management, and Permission denied error handling.
skill.summary: Shared skill for Lark CLI authentication, identity management, and permission handling with security best practices.
skill.author: larksuite

security_audit.summary: Static analyzer flagged 26 external_commands patterns and 1 weak cryptography pattern, all confirmed as FALSE POSITIVES. The flagged patterns are Markdown documentation code blocks (```bash), not executable Ruby/shell code. Line 4 flagged as weak crypto is actually a Chinese text description field. This skill contains only documentation with security best practices including prohibitions on outputting secrets and requiring confirmation for destructive operations.

low_findings.title: External Command Documentation
low_findings.description: Skill documents lark-cli bash commands for configuration and authentication. Commands are user-executed, not run by the skill itself.

content.user_title: Configure Lark CLI Authentication and Permissions
content.value_statement: This skill guides users through Lark CLI setup including authentication, identity switching between user and bot modes, and permission scope management. It provides clear error handling for permission issues and enforces security best practices.
actual_capabilities: Initialize Lark CLI application configuration with lark-cli config init
Guide users through authentication flow with lark-cli auth login
Switch between user identity and bot identity using --as flag
Handle Permission denied errors with appropriate scope recommendations
Provide security guidance for secret management and destructive operations
limitations: Cannot execute commands directly; provides documentation and guidance only
Requires users to have lark-cli installed on their system
Authentication must be completed by users through their own browser
Scope permissions must be configured in Lark developer console separately

use_cases.title: First-time Lark CLI Setup
use_cases.description: Guide new users through initial configuration and authentication setup for the Lark CLI tool.
use_cases.target_user: Developers integrating with Lark/Feishu platform for the first time

use_cases.title: Permission Error Resolution
use_cases.description: Help users diagnose and fix Permission denied errors by identifying missing scopes and providing fix commands.
use_cases.target_user: Developers encountering API permission issues during Lark integration

use_cases.title: Identity Mode Selection
use_cases.description: Assist users in choosing between user identity and bot identity based on their access requirements.
use_cases.target_user: Developers building Lark integrations requiring different access patterns

prompt_templates.title: Beginner: Initialize Configuration
prompt_templates.prompt: I need to set up Lark CLI for the first time. Guide me through the configuration process.
prompt_templates.scenario: New user starting with Lark CLI

prompt_templates.title: Beginner: Authenticate Login
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me log in to Lark CLI with the appropriate permissions for calendar access.
prompt_templates.scenario: User needs to authenticate for specific scope

prompt_templates.title: Intermediate: Fix Permission Error
prompt_templates.prompt: I got a Permission denied error when trying to access user calendars. The error mentions missing scopes. What should I do?

prompt_templates.title: Advanced: Choose Identity Mode
prompt_templates.prompt: I need to build an integration that accesses both user calendars and sends messages as an application. Should I use user identity or bot identity? Explain the differences.
prompt_templates.scenario: Architecting integration with mixed access requirements

output_examples.input: Guide me through Lark CLI initial setup
output_examples.output: To initialize Lark CLI, run the configuration command in background mode. The command will open an authorization link for you to complete in your browser. After you authorize, the CLI will be configured and ready to use.

output_examples.input: I got Permission denied when creating a calendar event
output_examples.output: The error indicates missing calendar write permissions. If using bot identity, add the required scope in Lark developer console. If using user identity, run the auth login command with the specific scope flag to request additional permissions.

best_practices: Always specify scope or domain when running auth login to follow least privilege principle
Use bot identity for application-level operations and user identity for accessing personal resources
Use --dry-run flag to preview potentially destructive operations before execution
anti_patterns: Never output appSecret or accessToken to terminal or logs in plain text
Do not run auth login for bot identity as bots do not require user authorization
Avoid performing write or delete operations without explicit user confirmation

faq.question: What is the difference between user identity and bot identity?
faq.answer: User identity (--as user) accesses your personal Lark resources like calendars and documents. Bot identity (--as bot) operates as the application itself and cannot access user personal resources but does not require user authorization.

faq.question: How do I fix a Permission denied error?
faq.answer: For bot identity, add the required scope in the Lark developer console. For user identity, run auth login with the --scope flag specifying the missing permission. Multiple login commands accumulate permissions.

faq.question: Do I need to run auth login for bot mode?
faq.answer: No. Bot identity only requires appId and appSecret configuration. The auth login command is only for user identity authorization.

faq.question: Can I switch between user and bot identity?
faq.answer: Yes. Use the --as user or --as bot flag with your commands to switch identity modes as needed for different operations.

faq.question: What should I do before running destructive commands?
faq.answer: Always confirm user intent before write or delete operations. Use the --dry-run flag to preview what the command will do without making actual changes.

faq.question: Why does my calendar query return empty results in bot mode?
faq.answer: Bot identity cannot access user personal calendars. Bot mode can only access resources owned by the bot application. Use user identity (--as user) to access personal calendars.
